jtbd.primerText: When [situation], I want [motivation], so I can [outcome]. Users hire products to make progress -- frame needs as jobs, not features.

evidence-quality.primerText: Evidence grades: anecdotal (1 report) to pattern (3+ signals) to quantified (metric-backed) to validated (tested). The grade determines your next move, not your confidence level.

switching-forces.primerText: Four forces explain adoption: Push (pain of current), Pull (appeal of new), Anxiety (fear of change), Habit (comfort of status quo). All four must be understood.

mom-test.primerText: Ask about past behavior, not future intentions. 'Would you use this?' is always wrong -- people are polite. Ask what they actually did.

pursue-monitor-defer.primerText: Every opportunity gets an explicit decision: pursue (invest now), monitor (watch for signals), or defer (not now, with a revisit trigger). Defer without a trigger is a silent discard.

given-when-then.primerText: Given [context], When [action], Then [outcome]. If you can't write it, you haven't defined it. Engineers know what to build, QA knows what to test.

non-goals.primerText: Explicit statements of what you're NOT building, with rationale. Without them, scope expands through 'while we're at it' thinking.

rice-ice-moscow.primerText: Reach x Impact x Confidence / Effort. Confidence must match evidence grade -- anecdotal=50%, pattern=80%, quantified=100%. Inflated confidence is self-deception.

kano-model.primerText: Basic needs (expected, anger if absent), Performance needs (more is better), Excitement needs (delight). Fix basics before building excitement -- you can't compensate for broken foundations.

north-star-metric.primerText: One metric capturing core value delivered to users. Not revenue (lagging). Must be: user-value, leading, team-influenced, simple, stable.

counter-metrics.primerText: Every target metric needs a guardrail. Optimizing scoring speed without a counter-metric for accuracy will sacrifice quality for speed.

hook-model.primerText: Trigger to Action to Variable Reward to Investment. External triggers build internal triggers. If the product only works with push notifications, the loop is weak.

cheapest-next-move.primerText: Always ask: what's the cheapest way to learn if this assumption is true? A $200 fake door test can prevent an $80K feature nobody wants.

build-measure-learn.primerText: Build the smallest thing, measure against pre-defined metrics, learn from data, build next thing. The loop runs continuously, not once.

assumption-mapping.primerText: List assumptions, rate on criticality times uncertainty. Test the ones that are both critical AND uncertain first.

slo-error-budgets.primerText: SLOs define acceptable failure rates; error budgets are the margin before an alert fires. Same guardrail thinking as counter-metrics -- both prevent optimizing one thing at the expense of another.

spike-prototype.primerText: A time-boxed investigation to reduce uncertainty before committing to a full build. Same risk-reduction pattern as cheapest-next-move in product validation.

test-coverage-confidence.primerText: Test coverage measures how much code is exercised by tests. Like evidence grading in product -- high coverage = higher confidence, but coverage alone doesn't guarantee correctness.

technical-debt.primerText: Shortcuts that were necessary at the time but now slow iteration. Product teams should care because it directly affects how fast new features can ship.

observability.primerText: The ability to understand system behavior from external outputs (logs, metrics, traces). Connects to product metrics -- you can't measure what you can't observe.

feature-flags.primerText: Toggles that control feature rollout independently of deployment. Enables product validation experiments (A/B tests) without requiring separate releases.

incident-response.primerText: Structured process for handling production failures. Connects to continuous improvement -- post-incident reviews produce the same insights as product retrospectives.

attribution-modeling.primerText: Determining which marketing touchpoints contributed to a conversion. Same evidence-quality challenge as product evidence grading -- correlation isn't causation.

conversion-funnel.primerText: The stages users pass through from awareness to purchase. Maps directly to AARRR pirate metrics -- both track drop-off between stages.

cac-ltv-ratio.primerText: Customer Acquisition Cost vs Lifetime Value. Connects to PLG strategy -- PLG aims to reduce CAC by making the product itself the growth engine.

positioning.primerText: How your product is perceived relative to alternatives. Connects to switching forces -- positioning addresses the Pull force (why switch to us).

channel-mix.primerText: Distribution of marketing investment across channels. Requires the same prioritization discipline as product backlog -- finite resources, infinite options.

acceptance-testing.primerText: Verifying that a feature meets its acceptance criteria. Directly connected to Given/When/Then -- acceptance tests ARE the executable version of acceptance criteria.

regression-testing.primerText: Ensuring new changes don't break existing functionality. Connects to continuous improvement -- the more you iterate, the more important regression safety becomes.

exploratory-testing.primerText: Unscripted testing to discover unexpected behaviors. Same discovery mindset as product problem discovery -- both seek unknown unknowns.

test-pyramid.primerText: Unit tests at the base, integration in the middle, E2E at the top. Connects to validation spectrum -- cheapest tests first, expensive tests for high-risk areas.

accessibility-testing.primerText: Verifying that products work for users with disabilities. Connects to ethical guardrails -- accessibility is a basic need (Kano), not an excitement feature.

edges.bridgeRationale: Both are guardrails that prevent optimizing one metric at the expense of another.
Both reduce uncertainty through the smallest possible investment before committing.
Both measure how confident you can be in a claim -- one about code, one about users.
Technical shortcuts compound like product debt -- both require deliberate improvement cycles.
You can't track product metrics without engineering observability -- they're two sides of measurement.
Feature flags are the engineering mechanism that makes product A/B tests possible.
Post-incident reviews and product retrospectives use the same structure: expected vs actual vs why vs next.
Attribution faces the same evidence-quality challenge -- distinguishing correlation from causation.
Marketing conversion funnels and AARRR pirate metrics track the same user journey from different lenses.
Product-led growth targets CAC reduction by making the product the acquisition engine -- the Hook Model is its behavioral mechanism.
Positioning addresses the Pull force -- why users should switch to your product.
Channel allocation requires the same prioritization discipline as product backlog -- finite resources, infinite options.
Acceptance tests are the executable version of Given/When/Then acceptance criteria.
Iteration velocity depends on regression safety -- both are part of sustainable improvement.
Both seek unknown unknowns through structured but open-ended investigation.
Both prioritize the cheapest validation first -- unit tests before E2E, desk research before beta.
Accessibility is a basic need in Kano terms -- users with disabilities expect it, absence is a failure.
